one_line_takeaway: For LLM agents controlling physical IoT, the deployment architecture and the (unauthenticated MQTT) transport it inherits determine security posture more than any model- or prompt-level mitigation: edge-local deployment kills structural cloud exfiltration but provides zero cryptographic provenance, silently leaks sovereignty under resource-exhaustion fallback, and opens multi-second unaudited failover windows.
key_finding: The full end-to-end actuation-to-audit pipeline (MQTT publish -> HA state read -> HA service call -> HA confirmation) has a mean delay of 116.7 ms (median 114.5 ms, P95 152 ms, N=150), of which the MQTT transport hop is only 31% (the total is 3.2x the MQTT-only figure); this is within the interception window of electromechanical actuators (door locks ~500 ms, valves ~1-2 s) but not solid-state relays (<5 ms). Provenance is complete under cooperation (100% metadata coverage over 100 commands) but has ZERO cryptographic enforcement: all four adversarial attacks (missing sender, spoofed sender, replayed message, direct-to-safety-topic publish) were accepted by the broker. Edge-local operation produced zero external egress (0 external IPs, 0 bytes over 280 operations) vs. the cloud OpenAI-API baseline (3 IPs, 64,981 bytes over 10 operations; 4.0x more bytes per normalized actuation), but a forced over-context input (109 KB) silently triggered fallback to the Anthropic cloud API (DNS queries to api.anthropic.com went 0->10, resolved IP 160.79.104.10, local model returned CANCELLED code 499) with NO user notification and no MQTT-layer anomaly — the sovereignty breach was visible only at the DNS layer. Failover from WiFi to an ADB bridge created a 35.7 s blackout window dominated by network-stack recovery (33.6 s), while MQTT reconnection in isolation was near-instantaneous (9.3 ms mean, P95 14 ms, N=50) — so the vulnerability window is a network/VPN-stack problem, not a messaging-layer one.
core_contribution: An empirical, systems-level security analysis of how DEPLOYMENT ARCHITECTURE (not model or prompt design) shapes the attack surface of LLM agents that control physical IoT devices. The authors' own three contributions are: (1) characterizing three deployment patterns as distinct threat models (cloud-hosted, edge-local swarm, hybrid) and enumerating five systems-level attack surfaces (S1a provenance forgery, S1b coordination-state divergence, S1c induced trust erosion, S2 silent sovereignty degradation, S3 actuation-audit temporal gap), two of which (S1b, S1c) they report as emergent failures they observed during live operation; (2) reframing security properties as measurable systems metrics — actuation-to-audit delay, provenance-chain completeness, data-sovereignty/egress, and failover vulnerability windows; and (3) empirical measurements from an operational MQTT-based edge agent swarm controlling real Home Assistant IoT hardware. The central claim is that unauthenticated MQTT pub/sub, inherited by default from the IoT ecosystem, becomes an insecure command-and-control plane when it carries autonomous agent commands to physical actuators. Note: model-level attacks (prompt injection, jailbreaking) and supply-chain attacks are EXPLICITLY EXCLUDED from scope (§2.3) — the cluster label 'LLM Agent Security' should not be read as making prompt injection their focus; it is the thing they carve out.
threat_model: Attacker is assumed to hold one or more of three capabilities (§2.3): (1) remote network access — e.g., compromised broker credentials or an exposed MQTT port; (2) physical access to an edge node — e.g., a stolen phone or USB/ADB debugging; (3) a rogue agent already inside the swarm — e.g., a compromised skill package installing a malicious MQTT client. Against these, the paper studies five systems-level attack surfaces that are architectural consequences of edge-local deployment: agent impersonation/command replay via unsigned MQTT envelopes (S1a), silent coordination-state divergence (S1b), induced trust erosion / denial-of-service-via-paranoia where forged traffic makes an agent distrust the whole channel and refuse operator commands (S1c), silent data exfiltration through legitimate cloud-inference fallback (S2), and unaudited actuation during failover blind spots (S3). Explicitly OUT OF SCOPE: model-level attacks (prompt injection, jailbreaking) and supply-chain attacks, which the authors treat as orthogonal and complementary. The single motivating real-world incident they cite (the ~7,000-unit DJI Romo robovac breach) is prior/cited work, not their own measurement.
limitations: Authors state (§4.6): a single three-node testbed with one broker (MQTT), one VPN substrate (Tailscale), and one IoT platform (Home Assistant) — generalization to other topologies/brokers/networks is future work. The cloud egress comparison (Table 4) is NOT workload-matched (the cloud baseline captures only inference API calls, not an equivalent full cloud-orchestrated workload) and is explicitly a 'directional baseline' rather than a controlled experiment. No mitigation prototypes (HMAC signing, topic ACLs, sovereignty-aware fallback) are implemented or evaluated. The three S1-family surfaces share a common root cause and could be viewed as one vulnerability. The failover measurement uses a WiFi->ADB bridge path (an Android debugging mechanism, not a typical consumer failover); the authors call the 35 s window a conservative upper bound since a WiFi->cellular failover would likely be faster. Reviewer-added caveat: the emergent failures (S1b/S1c) are single-run operational observations from the authors' own daily-use deployment, not statistically repeated trials.

datasets_used: No public dataset — a self-built, actively-used edge-local agent testbed (three nodes in the main text: Mac mini M4/Claude Opus 4.6, Moto G35/Gemini 3.1 Pro, Intel NUC N150/GPT-5.2; extended to five nodes in the appendix with a Pixel 4/Grok 4.2 and an observational Git-backed NUC11)
Real IoT hardware controlled via Home Assistant: 5 Philips Hue lights, 3 smart switches, 1 camera, 2 motion sensors, 1 speaker, 1 microphone
OpenClaw agent framework; MQTT pub/sub (broker port 1883) over a Tailscale/WireGuard mesh
Traffic/latency traces captured by the authors (tcpdump packet captures, microsecond-timestamp instrumentation) — not an external benchmark
quantitative_results: End-to-end actuation-to-audit delay: mean 116.7 ms, median 114.5 ms, P95 152 ms (N=150); stage breakdown MQTT publish 36.4 ms, HA state read 28.6 ms, HA actuation 27.6 ms (P95 64 ms, highest variance), HA confirmation 24.1 ms; MQTT hop = 31% of total, total = 3.2x MQTT-only
NUC->Mac mini MQTT path ~64 ms mean (N=50), 2.7x overhead reflecting node heterogeneity; burst load N=100 at 128 B: 49.8 ms mean, 0% degradation

Cooperative provenance: 100 commands across 5 action types, 100% metadata coverage on all fields; adversarial: all 4 broker attacks (missing sender, spoofed sender, replayed message, direct safety-topic publish) ACCEPTED

Data egress (10-min session): edge-local MQTT = 0 external IPs, 0 bytes, 280 ops vs. cloud OpenAI API = 3 IPs, 64,981 bytes, 10 ops; normalized per matched actuation ~805 B (edge) vs ~3,244 B (cloud) = 4.0x

Forced fallback (109 KB over-context input): DNS queries to api.anthropic.com 0->10 (resolved IP 160.79.104.10), packets to/from Percy 190->434, local model CANCELLED code 499; user notification = None, MQTT-layer anomaly = None
Failover: end-to-end 35.7 s from WiFi loss to first ping via ADB (network-stack recovery 33.6 s dominant); MQTT reconnection in isolation 9.3 ms mean (sigma 1.9 ms), P95 14 ms, P99 17 ms (N=50)
Fan-out latency (appendix, N=50 per config): 1 target 35 ms, 2 targets 41 ms, 4 targets 55 ms (~5 ms per added target, linear); broadcast constant 35 ms regardless of subscriber count
baselines_compared: No ML/model baselines — comparisons are architectural: cloud-hosted vs. edge-local swarm vs. hybrid deployment patterns
Cloud OpenAI-API egress vs. edge-local MQTT egress (Table 4)
Baseline operation vs. forced cloud fallback for sovereignty crossing (Table 5)
Sequential fan-out vs. single broadcast publish, and MQTT-reconnection-in-isolation vs. full network-stack failover (isolating the messaging layer from the network/VPN layer)
